proof: [§17] [15] Callias himself... for fear that his wealth would cost him his life, made over his property to the state in the event of his dying without issue.
reasoning: The text describes a legal mechanism where a citizen, fearing for his life due to his wealth, can legally designate the state as his heir to remove the incentive for his assassination by private parties.
specificity_reasoning: This applies to wealthy individuals without direct heirs who face specific threats of violence.

resource: Access to communal food resources
proof: [§114] ...but give also a part to those who lack. For some are contracting illness in the flesh by too much eating, and are injuring their flesh, and the flesh of the others who have nothing to eat is being injured by their not having sufficient food and their body is being destroyed.
reasoning: The text asserts a right for those who 'lack' or 'have nothing to eat' to receive a portion of the goods held by others. It identifies the physical destruction of the poor's bodies as a result of the rich withholding resources, implying the poor have a legitimate claim on the community's surplus.
specificity_reasoning: This applies to the broad class of the destitute or hungry within the society addressed.

resource: Right to receive charity without penalty
proof: [§190] Do good, and of all your toil which God gives you, give in simplicity to all who need, not doubting to whom you shall give and to whom not: give to all, for to all God wishes gifts to be made of his own bounties. Those then who receive shall render an account to God why they received it and for what. For those who accepted through distress shall not be punished, but those who accepted in hypocrisy shall pay the penalty.
reasoning: The text establishes a social rule within the community regarding the distribution of resources (alms/charity). It explicitly protects those in 'distress' from the social or divine 'penalty' associated with taking resources under false pretenses, effectively granting them a legitimate claim to community support.
specificity_reasoning: This rule applies broadly to the 'needy' or 'distressed' within the community, which constitutes a significant segment of the population addressed.

resource: Right to reside in central communal housing
proof: [§601] And those who gave up their sticks two-thirds dry, and one-third green, these are they who were faithful, but became rich and in honour among the heathen; then they put on great haughtiness and became high-minded, and abandoned the truth, and did not cleave to the righteous, but lived together with the heathen, and this way pleased them better. [...] For these, then, who became double-minded because of their deeds there is still repentance, but their repentance must be speedy that their dwelling may be within the tower. But for those who do not repent, but remain in their pleasures, death is near.
reasoning: The text describes a specific spatial organization of the community (the tower vs outside/death). Wealthy members who prioritize their riches and social status among 'the heathen' lose their right to 'dwell in the tower' (the symbol for the community of the saved), unless they quickly repent and divest from their haughtiness.
specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a specific socio-economic subset (the wealthy) within the religious community.

resource: Right to designate heirs by will
proof: [§75] So I thought of another clever move, and decided to make my will public. I’ve left him all my property in it, hoping he in turn would emulate me, and do the same by me.
reasoning: Cnemon's ability to publicly declare a will and designate a non-relative (Hermolaus) as his heir demonstrates that property owners have the concrete legal right to dispose of their goods via testament to whoever they choose.
specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to anyone in the society who possesses enough wealth to warrant a will, encompassing a large portion of the free population.
